title: Cat Abscess Draining Pus on Leg: Triage and Next Steps
meta_desc: Learn what to do if your cat has a draining abscess on its leg. Understand urgency levels, why photos help vets, and how to manage the wound.
content_html:
What is a Cat Abscess?

An abscess is a localized pocket of infection, usually containing pus, that forms under the skin. On a cat's leg, these are most commonly caused by bite wounds or scratches from other animals. When bacteria are trapped under the skin, the body's immune response creates a painful, swollen lump that eventually ruptures to release the infected fluid.

Is This an Emergency?

The urgency level for a draining abscess is categorized as Medium. While the fact that the abscess has burst often provides some immediate pressure relief for your cat, the underlying infection still requires professional medical treatment. You should contact your veterinarian to schedule an appointment within 24 hours. Seek immediate emergency care if your cat is extremely lethargic, has a high fever, or refuses to eat.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Taking a clear, well-lit photo of the wound can be incredibly helpful for your veterinary team. A photo allows the triage staff to assess the size of the opening, the color of the discharge, and the condition of the surrounding skin. This information helps them determine how quickly your cat needs to be seen and allows them to prepare the necessary equipment for cleaning and potentially placing a surgical drain.

faqs.question: Can I squeeze the pus out myself?
faqs.answer: No, you should never squeeze a cat's abscess. This can be extremely painful and may inadvertently push the infection deeper into the surrounding healthy tissue or even into the bloodstream.

faqs.question: What should I do until my vet appointment?
faqs.answer: Keep your cat indoors and in a clean environment. You can gently wipe away surface drainage with a clean, damp cloth, but avoid using harsh chemicals like hydrogen peroxide or alcohol, which can damage the tissue.

faqs.question: Will my cat need antibiotics?
faqs.answer: Yes, in almost all cases of a draining abscess, a veterinarian will prescribe a course of antibiotics to ensure the bacterial infection is completely eliminated and to prevent the abscess from reforming.
